topics.explanation: **G1 (Garbage First)** is the default collector since Java 9: divides the heap into equal-sized regions, prioritizes collecting regions with the most garbage, and targets a configurable pause-time goal (`-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis`). **ZGC** is a low-latency, mostly-concurrent collector (sub-millisecond pauses) that scales to multi-terabyte heaps using colored pointers and load barriers, trading some throughput for near-zero stop-the-world time. Tuning involves sizing young-gen, choosing pause-time targets, and monitoring via GC logs (`-Xlog:gc*`).

topics.label: memory leaks & profiling
topics.explanation: Memory leaks in Java happen despite GC when objects remain **reachable** unintentionally — e.g. static collections that grow unbounded, unclosed resources, listener/callback references never deregistered, or improper `equals`/`hashCode` causing `HashMap` bloat. Diagnose with heap dumps (`jmap`, `jcmd`) analyzed in **Eclipse MAT** or **VisualVM**, looking at retained-size dominator trees and GC-root paths. Profilers (async-profiler, JFR — Java Flight Recorder) reveal allocation hotspots and object retention over time.

topics.label: streams & functional Java
topics.explanation: The Streams API (Java 8+) enables declarative, pipeline-style processing: `stream().filter().map().collect()`. Streams are **lazy** (no work until a terminal op like `collect`/`reduce`/`forEach`) and can be sequential or `parallel()` (backed by the fork-join pool). Functional interfaces (`Function`, `Predicate`, `Supplier`, `Consumer`) plus lambdas/method references enable this style; `Collectors.groupingBy`/`toMap`/`joining` are the most-used terminal collectors. Side effects inside stream operations break referential transparency and should be avoided.

topics.label: exceptions, Optional
topics.explanation: Java exceptions split into **checked** (must be declared/caught, e.g. `IOException`, subclass of `Exception`) and **unchecked** (`RuntimeException` subclasses like `NullPointerException`, not enforced by the compiler) — `Error` represents unrecoverable JVM issues. `try-with-resources` auto-closes `AutoCloseable` resources. `Optional<T>` (Java 8) makes absence explicit in return types, replacing defensive null checks — use `map`/`orElseGet`/`orElseThrow`, but avoid `Optional` as a field type or method parameter.

topics.label: records, sealed classes, pattern matching, switch expressions
topics.explanation: **Records** (Java 16+) are immutable data carriers auto-generating constructor, accessors, `equals`/`hashCode`/`toString` from a compact field list (`record Point(int x, int y) {}`). **Sealed classes/interfaces** restrict which classes may extend/implement them (`sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Square`), enabling exhaustive `switch` pattern matching without a `default` branch. **Pattern matching** for `instanceof` and `switch` (Java 21) lets you deconstruct records directly: `case Point(int x, int y) -> ...`, reducing boilerplate casting code.

topics.label: Spring (IoC/DI, AOP, bean lifecycle, Boot auto-config, MVC, Data/JPA, @Transactional, Security)
topics.explanation: Spring's core is **IoC/DI**: the `ApplicationContext` instantiates and wires beans (constructor injection preferred over field injection for testability). **AOP** implements cross-cutting concerns (logging, transactions) via dynamic proxies/CGLIB weaving advice around join points. **Bean lifecycle**: instantiate → populate properties → `@PostConstruct` → ready → `@PreDestroy`. **Boot auto-configuration** uses `@Conditional` checks on the classpath to wire sensible defaults. `@Transactional` wraps a method in a proxy that begins/commits/rolls back a transaction (note: self-invocation bypasses the proxy). **Spring Data/JPA** generates repository implementations from interface method names; **Spring Security** layers filter chains for authn/authz.

topics.label: REST design
topics.explanation: REST design centers on resources identified by URIs, manipulated via HTTP verbs (`GET` idempotent/safe, `POST` create, `PUT` full replace/idempotent, `PATCH` partial update, `DELETE` idempotent) with status codes conveying outcome (`200`/`201`/`204`/`400`/`404`/`409`/`422`/`429`/`500`). Good design favors plural nouns, nesting only one level deep, pagination via cursors or `limit/offset`, versioning (URI or header-based), HATEOAS links where useful, and consistent error envelopes (`{code, message, details}`).

topics.label: testing (JUnit, Mockito)
topics.explanation: **JUnit 5** (Jupiter) structures tests with `@Test`, `@BeforeEach/@AfterEach`, `@ParameterizedTest`, and assertions (`assertEquals`, `assertThrows`). **Mockito** creates test doubles: `mock()` for stubbing collaborator behavior (`when(...).thenReturn(...)`), `@Mock`/`@InjectMocks` for DI into the class under test, and `verify()` to assert interactions. Good unit tests isolate the unit under test, avoid real I/O/DB calls, and follow Arrange-Act-Assert; integration tests use `@SpringBootTest` or Testcontainers for real dependencies.

modules.drill: Explain virtual threads vs platform threads; walk a GC pause you'd debug.

modules.title: Databases
modules.build: Tune `pgvector` + metadata queries with `EXPLAIN`; add a Redis cache before RAG retrieval.

topics.label: SQL (joins, aggregations, subqueries, CTEs, window functions)
topics.explanation: SQL joins combine rows across tables: `INNER` (matching only), `LEFT/RIGHT OUTER` (keep unmatched side with NULLs), `FULL OUTER`, and `CROSS` (cartesian product). Aggregations (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `GROUP BY`/`HAVING`) summarize rows; subqueries nest a query inside another (correlated subqueries re-run per outer row). **CTEs** (`WITH x AS (...)`) improve readability and allow recursion (`WITH RECURSIVE`). **Window functions** (`ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...)`, `RANK`, `LAG/LEAD`) compute per-row values across a related set without collapsing rows like `GROUP BY` does.

topics.label: indexing (B-tree, composite, covering, partial)
topics.explanation: A **B-tree index** stores sorted keys in a balanced tree, giving O(log n) lookups, range scans, and ordered traversal — the default for most RDBMS indexes. A **composite index** on `(a, b, c)` only helps queries filtering on a leading prefix of those columns (left-to-right rule). A **covering index** includes all columns a query needs, letting the engine answer purely from the index without touching the table (index-only scan). A **partial index** (e.g. `WHERE status = 'active'`) indexes only a subset of rows, reducing size and write overhead for highly skewed data.

topics.label: query optimization & EXPLAIN/plans
topics.explanation: Query optimizers choose an execution plan (scan type, join order, join algorithm) based on table statistics and cost estimates. `EXPLAIN` shows the planned/actual plan; `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` actually runs the query and reports real row counts and timing, exposing estimate-vs-actual mismatches that cause bad plans (often due to stale statistics). Look for **sequential scans** on large tables where an index scan was expected, high-cost **nested loop joins** on large unindexed sets, and missing/unused indexes — then add indexes, rewrite predicates to be index-friendly (avoid functions on indexed columns), or update table statistics (`ANALYZE`).

topics.label: N+1
topics.explanation: The **N+1 query problem** occurs when code fetches a list of N parent records, then issues one additional query per parent to fetch related child data (e.g. an ORM lazily loading `order.items` inside a loop over orders) — 1 + N round trips instead of a couple. Fix via eager loading (`JOIN FETCH` in JPQL, `.include()`/`.populate()` in other ORMs), batch fetching, or a single query with a `JOIN`/`IN (...)` clause. It's a top interview red flag when reviewing ORM-heavy code for performance.

topics.label: transactions & ACID
topics.explanation: A **transaction** groups operations into one atomic unit, governed by **ACID**: **Atomicity** (all-or-nothing, rollback on failure), **Consistency** (DB moves between valid states per constraints), **Isolation** (concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other's view, degree controlled by isolation level), **Durability** (committed data survives crashes, typically via write-ahead logging). `BEGIN`/`COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` delimit a transaction; savepoints allow partial rollback within one.

topics.label: isolation levels
topics.explanation: SQL isolation levels trade consistency for concurrency: **Read Uncommitted** (dirty reads possible), **Read Committed** (no dirty reads, but non-repeatable reads possible — Postgres/Oracle default), **Repeatable Read** (no dirty/non-repeatable reads, but phantom reads possible in theory — MySQL InnoDB default, though its MVCC implementation largely prevents phantoms too), and **Serializable** (full isolation, transactions behave as if run sequentially, at the cost of throughput via locking or serialization failures requiring retry).

topics.label: MVCC
topics.explanation: **MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)** lets readers see a consistent snapshot of data without blocking writers, by keeping multiple versions of a row tagged with transaction IDs/timestamps. Postgres keeps old row versions in the table itself (cleaned up by `VACUUM`); MySQL InnoDB keeps them in the undo log. This is how `Read Committed`/`Repeatable Read` achieve non-blocking reads — a writer creates a new row version rather than overwriting, and each transaction reads the version visible per its snapshot.

topics.label: locking & deadlocks
topics.explanation: Databases use **shared (read) locks** and **exclusive (write) locks** at row/table/page granularity to enforce isolation; `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` explicitly takes row locks. A **deadlock** occurs when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs, forming a cycle — the DB's deadlock detector picks a victim to abort and roll back. Avoid deadlocks by always acquiring locks in a consistent order, keeping transactions short, and indexing predicates to lock fewer rows (avoid full-table lock escalation).

topics.label: normalization vs denormalization
topics.explanation: **Normalization** (1NF→3NF/BCNF) eliminates redundancy by splitting data into related tables linked by foreign keys, preventing update anomalies but requiring joins. **Denormalization** intentionally duplicates data (e.g. storing a user's display name on every comment) to avoid joins and speed up reads, at the cost of write complexity and potential inconsistency. High-read, low-write systems (analytics, feeds) often denormalize; OLTP systems with frequent writes favor normalization, sometimes denormalizing only hot read paths.

topics.label: schema design
topics.explanation: Schema design starts from access patterns: identify entities, relationships, and cardinalities, then choose normalized tables for OLTP correctness or a denormalized/document shape for read-heavy NoSQL access. Decide primary keys (natural vs surrogate/UUID vs auto-increment — UUIDs avoid hotspotting in distributed systems but hurt index locality), foreign-key constraints for referential integrity, and which columns need indexes based on query filters. Always design with expected query patterns first, not just entity relationships.

topics.label: partitioning
topics.explanation: **Partitioning** splits one logical table into smaller physical pieces (partitions) on a single database instance — by range (e.g. date ranges), list, or hash — so queries that target a partition key prune to a fraction of the data, and maintenance (e.g. dropping old date partitions) is cheap. It improves query performance and manageability without distributing across servers, unlike sharding.

topics.label: sharding
topics.explanation: **Sharding** distributes data across multiple independent database instances (nodes), each holding a subset of rows determined by a shard key (hash-based, range-based, or directory-based). It scales writes horizontally beyond a single machine's limits but introduces cross-shard query/join complexity, the need for a routing layer, and rebalancing challenges when adding shards (consistent hashing mitigates this). Choosing a shard key with even distribution and locality for common queries is the central design challenge.

topics.label: replication (leader/follower, sync/async)
topics.explanation: **Replication** copies data from a leader (primary) to one or more followers (replicas). **Synchronous replication** waits for the follower to acknowledge before committing — guarantees no data loss on failover but adds latency and reduces availability if the follower is slow/down. **Asynchronous replication** commits on the leader immediately and ships changes afterward — better latency/availability but risks losing recent writes on leader failure. Followers typically serve read replicas to scale reads; leader failure requires promoting a follower (failover), which can cause brief unavailability or split-brain if mishandled.

topics.label: NoSQL types (document, key-value, wide-column, graph)
topics.explanation: NoSQL categories suit different access patterns: **document stores** (MongoDB) store JSON-like nested documents, good for flexible schemas and read-heavy aggregates; **key-value stores** (Redis, DynamoDB) offer O(1) lookups by key, ideal for caching/sessions; **wide-column stores** (Cassandra, HBase) organize data in column families optimized for write-heavy time-series/large-scale data with tunable consistency; **graph databases** (Neo4j) optimize traversal of densely connected relationships (social graphs, recommendation engines) that would require expensive joins in SQL.

topics.label: CAP & eventual consistency
topics.explanation: **CAP theorem** states a distributed system can only guarantee two of **Consistency** (all nodes see the same data), **Availability** (every request gets a response), and **Partition tolerance** (system works despite network splits) — and since partitions are inevitable, real systems choose **CP** (consistent but may reject requests, e.g. etcd, HBase) or **AP** (available but may serve stale data, e.g. Cassandra, DynamoDB). **Eventual consistency** is the AP compromise: replicas converge to the same value given enough time without new writes, used widely in distributed caches and NoSQL stores.

topics.label: caching (Redis, cache-aside, write-through, TTL, invalidation, stampede)
topics.explanation: **Redis** caching patterns: **cache-aside** (app checks cache, on miss reads DB and populates cache — most common, cache can lag DB) vs **write-through** (writes go to cache and DB synchronously, keeping them consistent but adding write latency). **TTL** bounds staleness by auto-expiring keys. **Invalidation** explicitly deletes/updates cache entries on writes to avoid stale reads. **Cache stampede** (a.k.a. thundering herd) happens when a hot key expires and many concurrent requests simultaneously hit the DB to repopulate it — mitigated with request coalescing/locking, probabilistic early expiration, or serving stale-while-revalidate.

topics.label: connection pooling
topics.explanation: **Connection pooling** (e.g. HikariCP) maintains a pre-warmed set of reusable DB connections instead of opening/closing a TCP+auth handshake per request, which is expensive. Pool size should be tuned to the DB's actual concurrency capacity (often `cores * 2` as a starting heuristic, per HikariCP's guidance) — oversized pools cause context-switching thrashing and contention on the DB side, not better throughput. Key settings: max pool size, connection timeout, idle timeout, and leak-detection threshold to catch connections never returned to the pool.

modules.drill: Read an EXPLAIN plan; pick isolation level for a given consistency need.

modules.title: Kafka & event-driven
modules.build: Re-architect Project 2's ingestion as a Kafka pipeline (producer → consumer chunks/embeds/indexes).

topics.label: architecture (broker, topic, partition, offset, replication, ISR, leader election)
topics.explanation: A Kafka **broker** is a server storing partitions of topics; a **topic** is a named stream split into **partitions** for parallelism, each an append-only log where each record gets a sequential **offset**. **Replication** copies each partition to multiple brokers for fault tolerance; the **ISR (in-sync replica)** set is the subset of replicas fully caught up with the leader. One replica per partition is the **leader** handling all reads/writes; if it fails, **leader election** (via the controller, using the Kafka Raft-based KRaft metadata quorum in modern versions) promotes an ISR member to leader.

topics.label: producers (acks, idempotence, batching, partitioning, keys)
topics.explanation: Producer **acks** controls durability: `acks=0` (fire-and-forget, fastest, can lose data), `acks=1` (leader-only ack, can lose data on leader failure before replication), `acks=all`/`-1` (all ISR replicas ack, strongest durability). **Idempotent producers** (`enable.idempotence=true`) attach a producer ID + sequence number so brokers dedupe retried sends, preventing duplicates from retries. **Batching** (`linger.ms`, `batch.size`) trades latency for throughput by grouping records per partition before sending. **Partitioning** is by explicit partition, by **key** hash (same key → same partition, preserving per-key order), or round-robin if no key.

topics.label: consumers (groups, rebalancing, offset commit, lag)
topics.explanation: Consumers join a **consumer group** to split partitions among members for parallel, exactly-once-per-group processing — each partition is consumed by exactly one member of a group at a time. **Rebalancing** reassigns partitions when members join/leave/fail (older eager rebalancing stops all consumption; newer cooperative/incremental rebalancing minimizes disruption). **Offset commits** (auto via `enable.auto.commit` or manual `commitSync`/`commitAsync`) track progress per partition; committing before processing risks message loss on crash, committing after risks duplicates. **Consumer lag** (difference between latest offset and committed offset) is the key health metric for a slow/stuck consumer.

topics.label: delivery semantics (at-most/at-least/exactly-once)
topics.explanation: **At-most-once**: commit offset before processing — messages can be lost if the consumer crashes mid-processing, but never reprocessed. **At-least-once**: commit offset after processing — messages may be reprocessed on crash/retry but never silently lost; this is the most common default and requires idempotent consumers. **Exactly-once**: achieved end-to-end via Kafka transactions (idempotent producer + transactional writes spanning produce-and-commit-offset atomically), typically within Kafka-to-Kafka pipelines (Kafka Streams) or via careful external sink design (e.g. upsert with a dedupe key).

topics.label: ordering guarantees
topics.explanation: Kafka guarantees **ordering only within a single partition** — records with the same key land on the same partition and are read in send order by the single consumer assigned to it. There is **no global ordering guarantee** across partitions. This is why choosing a good partition key (e.g. user ID, order ID) matters: it gives per-entity ordering while still allowing horizontal scale-out across many partitions for unrelated entities.

topics.label: transactions/EOS
topics.explanation: Kafka **transactions** (introduced with the Transactional API) let a producer write to multiple partitions/topics and commit them atomically, alongside committing consumer offsets in the same transaction — the basis for **EOS (Exactly-Once Semantics)** in read-process-write pipelines. Implemented via a transaction coordinator, producer epochs (fencing zombie producers), and marking records as committed/aborted with control messages that consumers (with `isolation.level=read_committed`) filter out if aborted.

topics.label: schema registry
topics.explanation: A **schema registry** (e.g. Confluent Schema Registry) stores versioned Avro/Protobuf/JSON-Schema definitions for topics, enforcing **compatibility rules** (backward, forward, full) so producers and consumers can evolve independently without breaking each other. Producers serialize with a schema ID embedded in the message; consumers fetch the schema by ID to deserialize. This decouples deploy order between producing and consuming services and catches incompatible schema changes at publish time instead of at runtime.

topics.label: Kafka Streams & Connect
topics.explanation: **Kafka Streams** is a client library for building stream-processing applications directly on Kafka (stateful operations like joins/aggregations backed by local RocksDB state stores and changelog topics for fault tolerance), without a separate cluster like Flink/Spark. **Kafka Connect** is a framework for source/sink connectors (e.g. Debezium CDC source, JDBC sink) that move data in/out of Kafka declaratively, scaling via distributed workers and handling offset tracking/restarts automatically.

topics.label: DLQ, retries, backpressure
topics.explanation: A **DLQ (dead-letter queue)** is a separate topic where messages that repeatedly fail processing are routed after exhausting retries, so the main pipeline isn't blocked and failures can be inspected/replayed later. **Retries** should use bounded backoff (exponential, with a retry topic/count header) rather than blocking the partition indefinitely. **Backpressure** is managed by tuning consumer `max.poll.records`/fetch sizes and pausing/resuming partitions, since Kafka itself buffers in the log — slow consumers just accumulate lag rather than crashing the producer side.

topics.label: outbox pattern
topics.explanation: The **outbox pattern** solves the dual-write problem (atomically updating a DB and publishing an event) by writing the event into an `outbox` table in the same local DB transaction as the business data change, then a separate process (CDC tool like Debezium, or a polling publisher) reads the outbox table and reliably publishes to Kafka, deleting/marking rows once sent. This guarantees the event is published if and only if the DB transaction committed, avoiding distributed transactions across the DB and message broker.

topics.label: Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS
topics.explanation: **Kafka**: high-throughput distributed log, partition-based parallelism, consumers pull and can replay/rewind via offsets, retains messages for a configurable period (not just until consumed) — best for event streaming, log aggregation, and replay scenarios. **RabbitMQ**: broker pushes messages to consumers, rich routing (exchanges/queues/topics), built for low-latency task queues with per-message ack semantics, but not designed for replay. **SQS**: fully managed AWS queue, simple push/pull model, at-least-once delivery, good for decoupling serverless workloads without operating infrastructure, but lacks Kafka's ordered partition/replay model (FIFO queues add limited ordering at lower throughput).

topics.label: event-driven patterns (event sourcing, CQRS, saga)
topics.explanation: **Event sourcing** stores state as an append-only sequence of domain events rather than mutable rows, rebuilding current state by replaying events — gives a full audit trail and time-travel debugging. **CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)** separates the write model (commands, often event-sourced) from read-optimized query models (materialized views), often kept in sync via the events. The **saga pattern** coordinates a multi-step distributed transaction across services using a sequence of local transactions plus compensating actions on failure, implemented either via orchestration (a central saga coordinator) or choreography (services react to each other's events).

modules.title: System design
modules.build: No single build for this module — it's drilled by rehearsing the canon out loud: URL shortener, news feed, chat, rate limiter, distributed cache, payment/ledger, notification service.

topics.label: fundamentals (scalability, availability, reliability, latency vs throughput, consistency)
topics.explanation: **Scalability** is the ability to handle growing load by adding resources (vertical = bigger machine, horizontal = more machines). **Availability** is the percentage of time a system is operational (measured in 9s, e.g. 99.9% = ~8.7h downtime/year). **Reliability** is the probability a system performs correctly over time without failure. **Latency** is the time for a single request to complete; **throughput** is requests handled per unit time — they're often in tension (batching raises throughput but adds latency). **Consistency** is whether all reads see the same up-to-date data, which trades off against availability/latency in distributed systems (CAP/PACELC).

topics.label: back-of-envelope estimation
topics.explanation: **Back-of-envelope estimation** quickly sanity-checks design feasibility: estimate QPS (daily active users × actions/user / 86400s), storage (records/day × record size × retention), and bandwidth (QPS × payload size). Use round numbers and powers of 2/10 (1 KB, 1 MB, 1 GB; 10^6 ≈ 1M; a day ≈ 86,400s ≈ 10^5s). Always state assumptions explicitly (e.g. "assume 100M DAU, 10 requests/user/day, read:write = 100:1") — interviewers care about the reasoning process and whether the resulting numbers correctly drive design decisions (e.g. "this needs sharding" vs "a single DB instance is fine"), not pinpoint accuracy.

topics.label: load balancing (L4/L7, algorithms)
topics.explanation: **L4 load balancing** operates at the transport layer (TCP/UDP), routing based on IP/port without inspecting payload — fast, protocol-agnostic. **L7 load balancing** operates at the application layer (HTTP), allowing routing by URL path, headers, or cookies, plus features like SSL termination and request-level retries. Common algorithms: **round-robin** (even rotation), **least connections** (route to least-busy backend), **weighted** (capacity-aware), and **consistent hashing** (sticky routing by key, minimizing redistribution when backends change — important for cache/session affinity).

topics.label: caching (layers, eviction, CDN)
topics.explanation: Caching layers exist at multiple levels: client/browser cache, **CDN** (edge caching of static/semi-static content close to users, reducing origin load and latency), reverse-proxy cache, application-level cache (Redis/Memcached), and DB buffer pool. **Eviction policies** decide what to drop when full: **LRU** (evict least-recently-used, most common), **LFU** (evict least-frequently-used), **FIFO**, or **TTL-based** expiration. Choosing the right layer and eviction policy depends on data volatility, hit-rate needs, and consistency tolerance.

topics.label: databases (SQL/NoSQL choice, sharding, replication, indexing)
topics.explanation: Choose **SQL** for structured data needing strong consistency, complex joins/transactions (orders, payments, inventory); choose **NoSQL** for flexible schemas, massive horizontal scale, or specific access patterns (key-value for sessions, document for catalogs, wide-column for time-series, graph for relationships). **Sharding** scales writes by splitting data across nodes by a shard key; **replication** (leader-follower) scales reads and provides failover. **Indexing** (B-tree, composite, covering) speeds reads at the cost of write overhead and storage — design indexes around the actual query patterns the system needs to serve.

topics.label: messaging & queues
topics.explanation: Message queues (SQS, RabbitMQ) and event streams (Kafka) decouple producers from consumers, absorb traffic spikes (acting as a buffer), and enable async processing so synchronous request paths stay fast. Use a **queue** for point-to-point task distribution/work queues (each message processed once by one worker); use a **pub/sub topic or event stream** when multiple independent consumers need the same event (notifications, analytics, audit, search indexing) or when ordered replay is needed (Kafka). Always design for idempotent consumers since most broker guarantees are at-least-once.

topics.label: consistency (CAP, PACELC, quorums)
topics.explanation: **CAP theorem**: under a network partition, choose Consistency or Availability (Partition tolerance is mandatory in any distributed system). **PACELC** extends this: even without a Partition, you still trade Latency vs Consistency (**ELC**) during normal operation — e.g. synchronous replication adds latency for consistency, async replication favors latency at the cost of staleness. **Quorums** (e.g. Dynamo-style `W + R > N`) let you tune the consistency/availability/latency balance per-operation: a write quorum `W` and read quorum `R` chosen such that reads always overlap with the latest write's replica set.

topics.label: API design (REST, gRPC, GraphQL, idempotency, pagination)
topics.explanation: **REST** uses resource-oriented URIs and HTTP verbs, simple and cacheable but can be chatty. **gRPC** uses HTTP/2 + Protobuf for low-latency, strongly-typed, streaming-capable service-to-service calls — common in internal microservice communication. **GraphQL** lets clients request exactly the fields they need in one round trip, avoiding over/under-fetching, at the cost of harder caching and more complex server-side resolution. **Idempotency** (critical for retries) is achieved via idempotency keys on `POST`/payment endpoints so a retried request doesn't double-charge. **Pagination** uses offset/limit (simple but slow/inconsistent on large or changing data) or cursor-based (stable, efficient for large datasets).

topics.label: rate limiting
topics.explanation: Rate limiting protects services from overload/abuse by capping requests per client/key per time window. Common algorithms: **token bucket** (tokens refill at a fixed rate, requests consume tokens, allows bursts up to bucket size), **leaky bucket** (smooths bursts into a constant output rate), **fixed window counter** (simple but allows 2x burst at window boundaries), and **sliding window log/counter** (more accurate, higher memory cost). Implemented centrally (API gateway, Redis with `INCR`+`EXPIRE` or a Lua script for atomicity) for distributed consistency across multiple service instances.

topics.label: microservices (gateway, service discovery, circuit breaker, saga, bulkhead)
topics.explanation: Microservices architecture uses an **API gateway** as the single entry point (routing, auth, rate limiting), **service discovery** (Consul, Eureka, or DNS-based in Kubernetes) so services find each other dynamically as instances scale, and a **circuit breaker** (e.g. Hystrix/Resilience4j pattern) that stops calling a failing downstream service after a failure threshold, failing fast and allowing recovery instead of cascading failure. The **saga pattern** handles distributed transactions across services via compensating actions. **Bulkhead** isolates resources (thread pools/connections) per dependency so one slow downstream service can't exhaust resources needed by others.

topics.label: data-intensive patterns
topics.explanation: Data-intensive patterns include **CDC (Change Data Capture)** for streaming DB changes into other systems (e.g. Debezium reading the WAL/binlog), **batch vs stream processing** tradeoffs (batch = higher latency, simpler correctness; stream = low latency, harder exactly-once semantics), the **lambda/kappa architecture** for combining both, **materialized views** for precomputed read-optimized projections, and **idempotent writes/dedup** as a baseline requirement whenever at-least-once delivery is involved anywhere in the pipeline.

topics.label: observability
topics.explanation: **Observability** rests on three pillars: **metrics** (numeric time-series like latency percentiles, error rate, QPS — Prometheus/Grafana), **logs** (structured, searchable event records — ELK/Loki), and **distributed tracing** (request-level spans across services showing where time is spent — Jaeger/Zipkin/OpenTelemetry). Good practice: correlate logs/traces with a request/trace ID propagated across service boundaries, alert on SLO burn rate rather than raw thresholds, and track the **four golden signals** (latency, traffic, errors, saturation).

topics.label: security
topics.explanation: System security spans **authentication** (verifying identity — OAuth2/OIDC, JWTs, mTLS for service-to-service), **authorization** (verifying permissions — RBAC/ABAC), **encryption** (TLS in transit, AES at rest), input validation/sanitization to prevent injection attacks, secrets management (Vault, KMS — never hardcoded credentials), and defense in depth at the network layer (VPCs, security groups, WAFs against common attacks like SQLi/XSS/CSRF/DDoS). Rate limiting and audit logging round out a baseline security posture for any production system design.

topics.label: AI system design (RAG platform, multi-tenant LLM gateway, agent orchestration with cost controls)
topics.explanation: AI-specific system design adds new concerns on top of classic distributed-systems patterns: a **RAG platform** needs a document ingestion/chunking pipeline, vector DB (pgvector/Pinecone/Milvus) for embedding similarity search, and a retrieval-then-generate flow with citation/grounding. A **multi-tenant LLM gateway** needs per-tenant API keys, rate limiting/quota enforcement, model routing (cost/latency-based fallback across providers), prompt/response caching, and token-usage metering for billing. **Agent orchestration with cost controls** requires bounding tool-call/reasoning loops (max iterations, timeout), budget caps per request/session, observability into token spend per step, and guardrails (output validation, human-in-the-loop escalation) to prevent runaway agent loops from spiraling cost or causing unsafe actions.

unlocks.answer:
**Clarify**: Custom short codes? Expected redirect latency? Analytics needed? Assume read-heavy (100:1 read:write), need 302/301 redirect choice (301 cacheable but skips analytics, so use 302).

**Estimate**: 100M new URLs/month (~40 writes/sec), 10B redirects/month (~4K reads/sec). Each record ~500 bytes → ~50GB/year, trivial for storage but read QPS needs caching.

**High-level components**: Client → Load Balancer → API service (write path generates code, read path resolves + redirects) → Cache (Redis) → Database (key-value store, e.g. DynamoDB) → async analytics pipeline (Kafka).

**Deep-dive**: Generate short codes via base62 encoding of an auto-incrementing ID (simple, no collisions) or a hash of the URL truncated + collision check. Distribute ID generation across nodes using a range-allocator (Snowflake-style) to avoid single point of contention. Store `{short_code, long_url, created_at, expiry, owner}`. Cache hot codes in Redis with TTL; cache-aside on miss. Use CDN/edge redirect for globally distributed low latency.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Hot-key reads handled by cache; custom alias collisions need a uniqueness check; choosing 302 over 301 trades client-side caching for accurate click analytics; database sharding by code prefix scales writes if growth exceeds single-instance capacity.

**Clarify**: Feed shows posts from followed users, ranked or chronological? Push or pull model? Scale: millions of users, celebrities with huge follower counts ("hot user" problem).

**Estimate**: 500M users, 100M DAU, average 200 follows/user, 10 posts/day system-wide per active user → billions of feed entries/day if fanned out naively.

**High-level components**: Post service (write), Fan-out service, Feed cache (Redis sorted sets per user), Graph/social service (follow relationships), Ranking service, CDN for media.

**Deep-dive**: Use **fan-out-on-write** (push new post ID into each follower's precomputed feed cache) for typical users — fast reads. For celebrities with millions of followers, switch to **fan-out-on-read** (pull their posts at request time and merge) to avoid a write storm — this hybrid approach is the standard answer. Feed cache stores sorted post IDs by time/score; actual post content fetched from a separate post store. Ranking can be a simple recency score or ML-based engagement score, computed by a separate ranking service merged at read time.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Fan-out write amplification for high-follower accounts is the core bottleneck, solved by the read/write hybrid; feed staleness vs cost tradeoff; storage growth requires TTL/archival of old feed cache entries.

**Clarify**: 1:1 and group chat? Delivery/read receipts? Online presence? Message persistence/history? Assume WhatsApp-scale, need low-latency real-time delivery plus offline message storage.

**Estimate**: 1B users, 100M concurrent connections, 50B messages/day (~580K msgs/sec average, higher at peak).

**High-level components**: Client (persistent WebSocket connection) → Gateway/connection servers (stateful, track which server holds which user's socket) → Message service → Message store (Cassandra/wide-column, partitioned by conversation ID) → Presence service → Push notification service for offline users.

**Deep-dive**: Connection servers maintain a mapping (in Redis) of `user_id → connection_server_id` so the message service can route a message to the right server holding the recipient's live socket; if offline, queue the message and trigger push notification. Messages stored per-conversation in append-only fashion with a monotonic sequence number for ordering and dedup. Group chats fan out to each member's queue. Use Kafka as the durable backbone for message delivery and to decouple ingestion from delivery, enabling retry and ordering per conversation.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Sticky connection routing limits horizontal rebalancing of gateway servers; message ordering only guaranteed per-conversation; read receipts/typing-indicators add chatty traffic, often handled best-effort over the same socket.

**Clarify**: Per-user, per-IP, or per-API-key limiting? Distributed across multiple service instances, so needs shared state. Assume a sliding-window-accurate limiter shared via Redis for an API gateway.

**Estimate**: 10K requests/sec across the fleet, limits like 100 req/min per user — state must be checked/updated on every request with sub-millisecond overhead.

**High-level components**: API Gateway → Rate Limiter Service (or embedded middleware) → Redis (centralized counter store) → backend services.

**Deep-dive**: Use **token bucket** algorithm: each user/key has a bucket with capacity C and refill rate R; on each request, atomically check-and-decrement via a Redis Lua script (atomicity is critical to avoid race conditions across distributed gateway instances). Alternative: **sliding window log** (store timestamps in a Redis sorted set, trim outside the window) for precise limiting at higher memory cost, or **sliding window counter** (weighted combination of current+previous fixed windows) as a practical middle ground. Return `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header when exceeded; expose limit/remaining/reset via response headers.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Redis becomes a single dependency for every request — mitigate with Redis Cluster and local in-memory pre-checks; fixed-window allows boundary bursts; sliding-window-log costs more memory per key; token bucket is the best balance of accuracy, burst tolerance, and cost.

**Clarify**: General-purpose distributed cache like Redis/Memcached itself — key-value, low latency, used by many services. Need eviction, replication, partitioning across nodes far exceeding single-machine memory.

**Estimate**: 1TB working set, 1M ops/sec, sub-millisecond latency target — single node insufficient, need a cluster of many nodes sharing the keyspace.

**High-level components**: Client (with smart client library) → Consistent-hashing router/proxy → Cache nodes (each holding a shard of the keyspace) → Replication for each shard (leader-follower) → Eviction manager per node.

**Deep-dive**: Use **consistent hashing** (hash ring with virtual nodes) to map keys to nodes, minimizing reshuffling when nodes are added/removed (only ~1/N keys move instead of all). Each shard has a leader handling writes, replicated async/sync to followers for read scaling and failover. **Eviction**: LRU (approximate LRU via sampling, like Redis's `volatile-lru`) when memory is full. Support TTL per key for natural expiry. For hot keys, add a thin client-side or L1 cache layer to avoid overloading a single shard. Gossip protocol or a coordination service (etcd/ZooKeeper) tracks cluster membership/topology.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Hot-key skew can overload one shard despite consistent hashing — fix via key splitting/replication of hot keys; consistency is eventual across replicas (acceptable for a cache); cache stampede on mass expiry needs jittered TTLs or request coalescing.

**Clarify**: Double-entry ledger semantics (every transaction = balanced debit+credit)? Idempotent payment processing avoiding double-charges? Strong consistency required — money cannot be lost or duplicated, unlike most other systems where eventual consistency is fine.

**Estimate**: 10M transactions/day (~115/sec average, bursty at peak), each transaction needs durable, auditable, immutable storage.

**High-level components**: Payment API (idempotency-key enforced) → Ledger service → Relational DB (strong ACID, e.g. Postgres) with double-entry tables → Async reconciliation/settlement workers → Event log (Kafka) for downstream notification/audit.

**Deep-dive**: Every financial event creates **at least two ledger rows** (debit one account, credit another) inserted in a single ACID transaction so the books always balance — never update balances in place; balance is derived by summing entries (immutable, auditable). Use an **idempotency key** per payment request stored with a unique constraint so retried client requests don't double-process. Use `SERIALIZABLE` or row-level locking on account rows for concurrent transfers to the same account, and a saga/outbox pattern for coordinating with external payment processors (Stripe-like) since that's a separate system requiring eventual consistency with compensating reversal entries on failure.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Strong consistency limits horizontal write scaling — mitigated by sharding ledgers per account/region; reconciliation jobs catch any drift versus external processor records; audit/immutability requirements rule out UPDATE-in-place balance columns.

**Clarify**: Channels needed — push, email, SMS, in-app? Priority/urgency tiers? Must tolerate provider (APNs/FCM/SES) failures and avoid duplicate/spam delivery. Assume multi-channel, high fan-out (e.g. marketing blast to millions) plus low-latency transactional notifications (OTP).

**Estimate**: 50M notifications/day, with bursty spikes (e.g. 1M within a minute for a broadcast) — needs async, queue-buffered processing rather than synchronous send.

**High-level components**: Notification API → Validation/templating service → Kafka (per-channel topics) → Channel-specific worker pools (Push worker, Email worker, SMS worker) → Third-party gateways (FCM/APNs, SES/SendGrid, Twilio) → Delivery-status tracker → User preference/opt-out service.

**Deep-dive**: Producers publish a notification event with `user_id, channel(s), template_id, payload` to Kafka; consumers per channel pull, render the template, look up user contact info/device tokens, respect user preferences/quiet hours, then call the third-party provider with retry+backoff and a DLQ for repeated failures. Idempotency keys prevent duplicate sends on retry. Rate limit per user to avoid notification fatigue. Track delivery status (sent/delivered/failed/opened) via provider webhooks feeding back into an event store for analytics.

**Bottlenecks/tradeoffs**: Third-party provider rate limits require backpressure/queuing on our side; broadcast spikes need worker autoscaling and topic partitioning by user-hash for parallelism; at-least-once delivery requires consumer-side idempotency and dedup.

modules.drill: Method: clarify → estimate → high-level → deep-dive one component → bottlenecks/tradeoffs. **Your edge:** fuse both tracks in one answer.

modules.title: DSA (background drip)
modules.build: Solve in Python (doubles as language fluency). ~3–5/week, daily in the final 2–3 weeks. Heavy only if the target tests it.

topics.label: arrays & strings
topics.explanation: Arrays/strings are contiguous, index-addressable (O(1) random access) but O(n) insert/delete in the middle. Recognize this pattern for problems involving in-place manipulation, prefix sums, or subarray/substring scanning. Canonical example: **"maximum subarray sum"** (Kadane's algorithm, O(n) single pass tracking running sum reset at negative dips).

topics.label: hashing
topics.explanation: Hashing (hash maps/sets) gives O(1) average lookup/insert by trading space for speed, ideal when you need to check membership, count frequencies, or find complements quickly without nested loops. Recognize it whenever a brute force is O(n²) due to repeated linear searches. Canonical example: **"two sum"** — store seen values in a hash map while scanning, checking `target - num` in O(1) instead of a nested loop.

topics.label: two pointers
topics.explanation: Two pointers move two indices through a (often sorted) structure to avoid nested loops, typically converging from both ends or advancing at different rates. Recognize it for sorted-array pair problems, palindrome checks, or merging. Canonical example: **"container with most water"** / pair-sum-in-sorted-array — move the pointer at the smaller value inward, since it's the limiting factor.

topics.label: sliding window
topics.explanation: Sliding window maintains a contiguous range `[left, right]` that expands/contracts to satisfy a constraint, avoiding recomputation from scratch for every substring/subarray. Recognize it for "longest/shortest/max subarray or substring satisfying X" problems. Canonical example: **"longest substring without repeating characters"** — expand right, shrink left when a duplicate is seen, tracking max window size, O(n).

topics.label: stacks & queues
topics.explanation: Stacks (LIFO) suit problems needing to track "most recent unmatched" state — parentheses matching, undo history, DFS iterative traversal. Queues (FIFO) suit ordered processing — BFS, task scheduling. Recognize stacks for nested/matching structure problems and monotonic-stack problems (next greater element). Canonical example: **"valid parentheses"** — push opening brackets, pop and match on closing brackets, valid iff stack empties.

topics.label: linked lists
topics.explanation: Linked lists allow O(1) insertion/deletion at a known node but O(n) access, and require careful pointer manipulation (dummy head nodes, tracking `prev`/`curr`/`next`). Recognize this pattern for problems involving reversal, cycle detection, or merging without extra array space. Canonical example: **"detect a cycle"** (Floyd's cycle detection / tortoise-and-hare) — slow and fast pointers meet iff a cycle exists; finding the cycle start requires a second phase from head.

topics.label: trees & BST
topics.explanation: Trees model hierarchical data; a **BST** keeps left-subtree < node < right-subtree, giving O(log n) search/insert/delete when balanced (O(n) worst case if degenerate/unbalanced, motivating self-balancing variants like AVL/red-black). Recognize tree recursion for hierarchical traversal (preorder/inorder/postorder/level-order/BFS) problems. Canonical example: **"validate a BST"** or **"lowest common ancestor"** — recursive traversal carrying min/max bounds or comparing target values against the current node.

topics.label: heaps/priority queues
topics.explanation: A heap (binary heap, usually array-backed) maintains the min or max element accessible in O(1) with O(log n) insert/extract — ideal whenever you repeatedly need the current smallest/largest element from a changing set. Recognize it for "top-K", "k-th largest", merge-k-sorted-lists, or scheduling-by-priority problems. Canonical example: **"k-th largest element in a stream"** — maintain a min-heap of size k; if a new element exceeds the heap's minimum, replace it.

topics.label: tries
topics.explanation: A trie (prefix tree) stores strings character-by-character along tree edges so all strings sharing a prefix share a path, giving O(L) lookup/insert (L = string length) independent of how many strings are stored. Recognize it for autocomplete, prefix search, or dictionary/word-validation problems. Canonical example: **"implement autocomplete / word search II"** — build a trie of the dictionary, then traverse the board/input matching trie edges to prune search early.

topics.label: graphs (BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, union-find, topological sort)
topics.explanation: Graph algorithms: **BFS** explores level-by-level (shortest path in unweighted graphs, uses a queue); **DFS** explores depth-first (uses a stack/recursion, good for connectivity, cycle detection, topological sort); **Dijkstra** finds shortest paths in weighted graphs with non-negative edges using a min-heap greedy relaxation; **union-find (disjoint set)** efficiently tracks connected components with near-O(1) amortized union/find via path compression + union by rank; **topological sort** (Kahn's algorithm or DFS post-order) orders nodes in a DAG respecting dependencies. Canonical example: **"course schedule"** (topological sort to detect/resolve dependency cycles) or **"network delay time"** (Dijkstra).

topics.label: recursion & backtracking
topics.explanation: Recursion solves a problem by reducing it to smaller subproblems of the same shape, with a base case to terminate; backtracking adds an explicit "try → recurse → undo" loop to explore a decision tree of choices, pruning branches that can't lead to a valid solution. Recognize backtracking for "generate all combinations/permutations/subsets" or constraint-satisfaction problems (N-Queens, Sudoku). Canonical example: **"permutations"** or **"N-Queens"** — place a choice, recurse, then remove it (backtrack) before trying the next choice.

topics.label: binary search
topics.explanation: Binary search finds a target (or boundary) in O(log n) over a **sorted or monotonic** search space by repeatedly halving the range based on a comparison. Recognize it whenever the answer space is monotonic even if the input array itself isn't literally sorted ("binary search on the answer" — e.g. minimizing a max value subject to a feasibility check). Canonical example: **"search in rotated sorted array"** or **"find minimum capacity to ship packages within D days"** (binary search on answer + feasibility check).

topics.label: sorting
topics.explanation: Know complexity/stability/in-place tradeoffs: **quicksort** (O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst, in-place, not stable), **mergesort** (O(n log n) guaranteed, stable, O(n) extra space — good for linked lists/external sorting), **heapsort** (O(n log n), in-place, not stable), and when a problem only needs partial ordering, **quickselect** finds the k-th order statistic in O(n) average without fully sorting. Canonical example: **"k-th largest element"** via quickselect, or "sort colors" (Dutch national flag, one-pass three-way partition).

topics.label: greedy
topics.explanation: Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping it leads to a globally optimal solution — valid only when the problem has the **greedy-choice property** and **optimal substructure** (provable via exchange argument), otherwise greedy gives a wrong/suboptimal answer where DP would be needed. Recognize it for interval scheduling, and resource-allocation-by-sorting problems. Canonical example: **"activity/interval scheduling"** — sort by end time, greedily pick the next non-overlapping interval, proven optimal by exchange argument.

topics.label: dynamic programming (1-D, 2-D, knapsack, LIS, edit distance)
topics.explanation: DP solves problems with **overlapping subproblems** and **optimal substructure** by caching subproblem results (memoization top-down, or tabulation bottom-up), avoiding exponential recomputation. **1-D DP** tracks state over a single dimension (Fibonacci, climbing stairs); **2-D DP** tracks two dimensions (grid paths, edit distance); **knapsack** (0/1 vs unbounded) decides item inclusion under a capacity constraint; **LIS** (longest increasing subsequence) finds the longest monotonic subsequence (O(n²) DP or O(n log n) with binary search); **edit distance** computes minimum insert/delete/replace operations between two strings via a 2-D table. Canonical example: **"edit distance"** or **"0/1 knapsack"**.

topics.label: intervals
topics.explanation: Interval problems involve ranges `[start, end]` that need merging, overlap-checking, or scheduling — the standard technique is sorting by start (or end) time, then sweeping through with a running comparison against the previous interval's end. Recognize it for "merge overlapping meetings", "insert interval", or "minimum rooms needed" problems. Canonical example: **"merge intervals"** — sort by start time, then merge the current interval into the last result if they overlap (`current.start <= last.end`).

topics.label: bit manipulation
topics.explanation: Bit manipulation uses AND/OR/XOR/shift operations for O(1) per-bit tricks: XOR cancels duplicate pairs (`a ^ a = 0`), `n & (n-1)` clears the lowest set bit (useful for counting set bits or checking power-of-2), and bitmasks compactly represent subsets for DP-over-subsets problems. Recognize it for "find the single/unique number", subset enumeration, or space-optimized state representation. Canonical example: **"single number"** — XOR all elements; every paired value cancels to 0, leaving only the unpaired one.

topics.label: Big-O time/space analysis
topics.explanation: **Big-O** describes the worst-case asymptotic growth rate of time/space as input size n grows, ignoring constants and lower-order terms — e.g. O(n) linear scan, O(log n) binary search, O(n log n) efficient sorting, O(n²) nested loops, O(2^n) exponential brute-force subsets. In interviews, always state both time **and space** complexity, account for recursion stack depth as space, and distinguish average-case from worst-case (e.g. quicksort O(n log n) average but O(n²) worst case on adversarial input).

modules.drill: Pick one pattern above at random and solve + explain the approach out loud before coding.

modules.title: Behavioral round
modules.weeks: Prep now → ramp in final 3 weeks
modules.build: No artifact to build here — rehearse the stories behind the artifacts you already shipped (RAG service, agent+eval harness, QLoRA run, live deployment) using the STAR method.

topics.label: STAR method
topics.explanation: **STAR** = Situation, Task, Action, Result. Interviewers use it to score behavioral answers, so structure yours the same way: 1–2 sentences of context, the specific thing you owned, what you actually did (the bulk of the answer), and a measurable outcome. Skipping straight to "what I did" without the Situation/Task setup is the most common reason a good story lands flat.

topics.label: quantified results ("p95 4s → 900ms")
topics.explanation: Vague answers ("I improved performance") get discounted; numbers make a story verifiable and memorable. Always have the before→after pair ready — latency (`p95 4s → 900ms`), cost (`$0.08 → $0.02 per request`), error rate, or adoption. If you don't have an exact number, give a defensible estimate and say so ("roughly 4x, I didn't log the exact baseline") rather than inventing false precision.

topics.label: ownership/shipping stories
topics.explanation: An ownership story shows you drove something end-to-end without being told every step — you noticed a gap, made a call, and saw it through to shipped/measured impact. Pick a story where you made a non-obvious decision (not just executed a spec), since that's what differentiates "did the work" from "owned the outcome."

topics.label: a confident-then-wrong story (show the debugging path)
topics.explanation: This is a deliberately uncomfortable but high-value story: a time you were confident in an approach, it turned out wrong, and you had to debug your own assumption. Interviewers ask for this to test intellectual honesty and debugging process, not to catch you out — narrate the wrong hypothesis, the signal that contradicted it, and how you pivoted, rather than glossing over the wrong part.

topics.label: a production incident diagnosed via traces
topics.explanation: A concrete incident you diagnosed using traces/logs/dashboards rather than guesswork: what alerted you, what the trace showed, root cause, fix, and the follow-up (postmortem, alert, regression test) that prevents recurrence. This doubles as your "I operate production systems" evidence — pick one with a real trace/span/log line you can describe specifically.

topics.label: a cost-vs-latency-vs-quality tradeoff
topics.explanation: A genuine three-way tradeoff you navigated explicitly — e.g. a smaller/cheaper model plus caching to hit a cost target while keeping p95 latency and answer quality acceptable. The strong version of this story names the metric you protected, the one you sacrificed, and why, rather than claiming you optimized all three at once (which reads as not having actually faced a tradeoff).

topics.label: GenAI safety/reliability approach
topics.explanation: Your point of view on shipping GenAI responsibly: layered defenses (input validation, output validation, PII/jailbreak detection), human-in-the-loop for risky actions, eval gates before deploy, and monitoring for drift/silent decay in production. Ground this in something you actually built (a guardrail, an eval gate, an approval step) rather than reciting a generic safety checklist.

topics.label: conflict resolution
topics.explanation: A disagreement — with a teammate, a PM, or a reviewer — that you resolved professionally: what the disagreement was actually about (often a hidden difference in assumptions or priorities), how you surfaced that, and the resolution. The best version shows you changed your mind or found a third option, not just "I explained my view and they agreed."

topics.label: learning a new domain fast
topics.explanation: Evidence you can ramp on unfamiliar territory fast — exactly what this whole syllabus is proof of. Have a concrete example of picking up a new domain (a new framework, a new part of the stack, or GenAI itself) and describe your method: how you scoped the unknowns, what you built first to de-risk it, and how quickly you became productive.

unlocks.text: Tell the story of the end-to-end live LLM system you shipped
unlocks.answer: I shipped a production RAG service end-to-end — ingestion through a chunking and embedding pipeline, hybrid dense+BM25 retrieval with reciprocal rank fusion, a cross-encoder reranker, and generation with citations, fronted by a FastAPI service with streaming responses. I deployed it Dockerized with CI/CD, rate limiting, retries, and prompt-injection/PII guardrails, and kept it running as a public demo rather than letting it die after the build. The part I'm proudest of: reranking moved NDCG@10 measurably (I track this number, not just "it felt better"), and I added an eval harness with a versioned golden set so I'd catch regressions instead of shipping by vibe. End-to-end ownership — data pipeline, retrieval quality, latency budget, cost per query, and guardrails — is the story, not just "I called an LLM API."

unlocks.text: Walk through an incident you debugged
unlocks.answer: I noticed [latency/error-rate/cost] spike via my dashboard's p95/p99 alert, pulled the trace for a slow request, and followed the span tree from the API gateway through retrieval into the LLM call. The trace showed the time was sitting in vector search, not the model call, which ruled out my first hypothesis (rate limiting on the LLM provider). Digging into the index, the root cause was an un-warmed HNSW index after a redeploy, causing a fallback to a slower exact scan. I fixed it by adding an index-warm health check that gates traffic until the index reports ready, then added a regression test and a dedicated latency alert on retrieval specifically (not just end-to-end) so the same failure mode pages faster next time. The fix took the affected p95 from several seconds back under 1s, and I wrote a short postmortem so the warm-check became a standard part of the deploy checklist.

unlocks.text: Describe a hard tradeoff you made
unlocks.answer: On my RAG service, I had to choose between a larger model for better answer quality and a smaller/cheaper model with more aggressive caching and a reranker to compensate. I protected latency and cost — semantic caching plus a cheaper model and reranking got me most of the quality gain at a fraction of the per-query cost — and explicitly accepted a small hit on the hardest long-tail queries, which I routed to the larger model only when the reranker's confidence was low. That routing rule was the actual tradeoff: I didn't pretend I could have max quality, min cost, and min latency simultaneously; I picked which two mattered for this use case and built the system to fail gracefully on the third. I'd quantify it as roughly a 4x cost reduction with a small, measured drop in answer relevance on the easiest 80% of queries, recovered selectively on the hard 20%.

On a fine-tuning project with sensitive data, a teammate (or reviewer) wanted to fine-tune directly on raw production logs for speed; I pushed back because that risked baking PII into model weights with no easy way to redact afterward. The disagreement wasn't really about speed vs. safety in the abstract — it was about who'd own the risk if it leaked, which hadn't been made explicit. I proposed a middle path: a PII-scrubbing and provenance-tracking step in the data pipeline before training, which added a day of work but kept the timeline mostly intact and gave us an audit trail. We went with that, and it became the standard data-prep step for later fine-tunes too — the resolution stuck because it solved the underlying concern (auditable, excludable data) rather than just splitting the difference on time.

I switched to AI because the highest-leverage engineering work is moving there, and I wanted to be building it rather than watching from the core-engineering side. I didn't take this lightly: I spent [N] weeks/months building real, working systems — a production RAG service, an agent with tool calling and MCP integration, an evaluation harness, a QLoRA fine-tune, and a live deployed service — specifically so I'd have shipped, debugged, and operated GenAI systems rather than just read about them. My core engineering background (Java, databases, distributed systems, Kafka) isn't dead weight here — it's what lets me reason about latency budgets, consistency, and failure modes in an AI system the same way I would for any production system, which is exactly the gap a lot of AI-only candidates have.

modules.drill: Tell your "why the switch to AI" story and one production incident, each in under 90 seconds, with a quantified result.

notes.capstone:
Your shipped artifacts are arranged so one project answers many rounds:

- **RAG service** → LLM fundamentals, RAG architecture, vector DBs, system design, behavioral.
- **Agent + eval harness** → agents, MCP, evaluation, observability, failure modes.
- **QLoRA run** → fine-tuning, RAG-vs-finetune, security/compliance.
- **Live deployment** → serving, cost/latency, guardrails, the "I operate production AI" story.

Every answer starts with "When I built X, I hit exactly this — here's what I did and the number it moved." That sentence wins the ₹25–50 LPA band.
notes.finalWeeks:
- **Daily:** 2 system-design or RAG/agent-design drills out loud.
- **Mocks:** 2–3/week (one AI technical, one system design, one core-eng) — peer or LLM as interviewer.
- **Flashcards:** run your own TOPICS + UNLOCKS lists; any gap → reopen the build and break/fix that part.
- **Per company:** map the JD to these modules; rehearse the 3 likeliest questions.
- Keep the live demo running and READMEs sharp — interviewers click them.
